stories.headline: NASA's Europa Clipper Sends Back First Close-Up Data from Jupiter's Icy Moon
stories.summary: NASA's Europa Clipper spacecraft, which has been journeying toward Jupiter since its October 2024 launch, has begun transmitting detailed magnetic field and particle data from its early flybys of Europa — a moon scientists believe harbors a vast liquid ocean beneath its frozen crust. The data is helping researchers map the thickness of the ice shell and search for chemical signatures that could indicate conditions friendly to life. This matters enormously because Europa is considered one of the top candidates in our solar system for finding extraterrestrial life, even if microbial.
stories.bigWordDef: The scientific study of whether life could exist elsewhere in the universe, including what conditions life needs to survive.
stories.thinkQuestion: If we discovered single-celled microbial life in Europa's ocean, how do you think that discovery would change the way humans think about our place in the universe — and what responsibilities would we have toward that life?
stories.videoQuery: NASA Europa Clipper mission Jupiter moon ocean explained

stories.headline: India and Pakistan Tensions Escalate After Pahalgam Terror Attack Kills 26 Tourists
stories.summary: A deadly terrorist attack in the Pahalgam area of Jammu and Kashmir on April 22 killed 26 civilians, mostly tourists, triggering the worst diplomatic crisis between India and Pakistan in over a decade. India has suspended the Indus Waters Treaty, expelled Pakistani diplomats, and closed the Wagah border crossing, while Pakistan has retaliated by closing its airspace to Indian aircraft. The crisis is being watched closely by global powers because both countries are nuclear-armed, making any miscalculation potentially catastrophic for the entire region.
stories.bigWordDef: When a conflict or argument keeps getting bigger and more serious, with each side responding more strongly than the last.
stories.thinkQuestion: When two nuclear-armed countries are in a serious conflict, what role should the international community play — and is there a risk that outside involvement could make things worse rather than better?
stories.videoQuery: India Pakistan Pahalgam attack 2025 Kashmir conflict explained

stories.headline: India Launches Its Largest-Ever Rooftop Solar Programme, Targeting 10 Million Homes
stories.summary: The Indian government announced it has crossed the milestone of enrolling 5 million households in PM Surya Ghar, its flagship free rooftop solar scheme, and has doubled its target to 10 million homes by 2027. Participating families receive subsidized solar panels that cut electricity bills to near zero and can sell surplus power back to the grid, earning extra income. For a country where summer temperatures are now regularly hitting record highs and electricity demand is surging, the programme is both a climate solution and an economic lifeline for middle-class families.
stories.bigWordDef: The point where solar energy costs the same or less than electricity from traditional power stations, making it just as practical to use.
stories.thinkQuestion: Rooftop solar gives power and income to individual families, but large solar farms can generate power for entire cities — which approach do you think India should prioritize, and why might the answer be different for rural versus urban areas?
stories.videoQuery: India PM Surya Ghar rooftop solar scheme how it works

stories.headline: Global Coral Bleaching Crisis Declared a Planetary Emergency as Fourth Mass Bleaching Event Confirmed
stories.summary: The US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) and the International Coral Reef Initiative have confirmed that the world is in the middle of its fourth global mass coral bleaching event, with record ocean temperatures causing corals to expel the algae that give them colour and nutrients — a process that kills them if temperatures stay high for too long. Over 80 percent of the world's reef areas have now experienced bleaching-level heat stress, including large sections of Australia's Great Barrier Reef, India's Lakshadweep reefs, and reefs across the Pacific. Coral reefs support roughly 25 percent of all marine species and provide food and coastal protection for over a billion people, making this one of the most consequential ecological emergencies of our time.
stories.bigWordDef: A relationship between two different living things where they both benefit from each other — like coral and the tiny algae that live inside it and feed it through sunlight.
stories.thinkQuestion: Coral reefs are sometimes called 'the rainforests of the sea' — if we cannot stop ocean temperatures from rising fast enough to save them, should we invest in artificial reef technology or focus entirely on cutting emissions, or both, and how would you decide how to split the resources?
stories.videoQuery: coral bleaching 2024 2025 global crisis explained oceans

stories.headline: Carlos Alcaraz Wins the Monte-Carlo Masters, Cementing Himself as World Number One Ahead of Roland Garros
stories.summary: Spanish tennis star Carlos Alcaraz won the Monte-Carlo Masters clay-court title in April 2026, defeating Jannik Sinner in a thrilling three-set final that lasted nearly three hours, a result that re-established Alcaraz as the world's top-ranked player with the French Open just weeks away. The rivalry between 23-year-old Alcaraz and 24-year-old Sinner is being compared by tennis historians to the legendary Federer-Nadal era for its intensity and quality. For Indian tennis fans, the match also highlighted the rise of Sumit Nagal, who reached the quarterfinals — the deepest run by an Indian man at a Masters 1000 event in nearly two decades.
stories.bigWordDef: An ongoing competitive relationship between two people or teams who are very evenly matched and keep pushing each other to be better.
stories.thinkQuestion: Sports rivalries like Alcaraz versus Sinner or Federer versus Nadal seem to make both players perform better than they might alone — what is it about competition that brings out excellence, and can you think of examples in fields outside sport where the same thing happens?
stories.videoQuery: Carlos Alcaraz Monte Carlo 2026 highlights Sinner

stories.headline: Scientists Grow Functional Human Kidney Tissue Using Stem Cells for the First Time
stories.summary: Researchers at the Murdoch Children's Research Institute in Melbourne, Australia published findings in April 2026 showing they had successfully grown small but functional kidney organoids — miniature kidney-like structures from human stem cells — that can filter waste the way a real kidney does, a world first. While these organoids are far too small to transplant into patients yet, the breakthrough gives scientists a working model to test new drugs, study kidney diseases, and eventually work toward lab-grown kidneys for the 850 million people worldwide living with chronic kidney disease. India has one of the highest rates of chronic kidney disease in the world, partly linked to diabetes and hypertension, making this research particularly relevant for the country.
stories.bigWordDef: A tiny, simplified version of an organ grown in a laboratory from stem cells — it behaves like the real organ but is much smaller and cannot replace it yet.
stories.thinkQuestion: If lab-grown organs become a reality in the next 20 years, they could eliminate transplant waiting lists — but they would also raise deep ethical questions about what it means to grow human body parts in a lab. What ethical boundaries do you think scientists and governments should set around this technology?
stories.videoQuery: kidney organoid stem cells lab grown organ breakthrough explained

videos.title: NASA Europa Clipper: Hunting for Life in Jupiter's Hidden Ocean
videos.description: An explainer covering how Europa Clipper works, why Europa's subsurface ocean is so exciting, and what scientists hope to find beneath the ice.

videos.title: The Global Coral Bleaching Crisis: What Is Killing the World's Reefs?
videos.description: A visually striking deep-dive into why ocean temperatures are bleaching coral reefs worldwide and what losing them would mean for marine life and human communities.

videos.title: Stem Cells to Organs: How Scientists Are Growing Human Body Parts in a Lab
videos.description: Covers the science of stem cells and organoids, explaining how researchers are inching toward lab-grown kidneys and other organs and why it matters for medicine.

infographic.caption: The Scale of the Global Coral Crisis — By the Numbers
infographic.fact: Over 80% of the world's reef areas affected by bleaching heat stress in 2025–26 · Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor but support 25% of all marine species · More than 1 billion people depend on reefs for food and coastal protection · Ocean surface temperatures in 2025 were the highest ever recorded for the fourth consecutive year · India's Lakshadweep reefs saw bleaching alerts for 11 consecutive weeks in early 2026
infographic.question: If coral reefs disappeared within your lifetime, what would be the chain of effects — and which communities around the world, including in India, do you think would feel it first and most severely?

quiz.q: What makes Europa, one of Jupiter's moons, so exciting to scientists searching for life?
options: A It has an atmosphere full of oxygen
B It is believed to have a liquid water ocean beneath its icy surface
C It is the closest moon to Earth
D Its surface temperature is warm enough for liquid water on top of the ice
A Coral turns white because sunlight bleaches its colour over time
B Coral expels the algae living inside it due to warm water stress, losing colour and nutrition
C Chemical pollution paints coral white
D Coral dies instantly when ocean temperatures rise

quiz.q: What is an organoid, as used in the new kidney research?
options: A A full-sized artificial kidney ready for transplant
B A computer model simulating how a kidney works
C A tiny lab-grown structure made from stem cells that mimics an organ's function
D A robot designed to clean kidneys during surgery

B The Lahore Declaration
C The Indus Waters Treaty
D The Tashkent Agreement
